blocks.title: Overview
blocks.text:
One of the most paralyzing misconceptions in ministry is the belief that resource development means fundraising — asking people for money they do not want to give for a cause they do not fully understand. This session systematically dismantles that misconception by reframing resource development as cause-based relationship building. The DVULI framework, grounded in the Discover Total Resources guide and Simon Sinek's Golden Circle (Why, How, What), reveals that people do not give to organizations — they give to causes they believe in, communicated by leaders they trust, through relationships they have built. The resources that flow from those relationships are far more than money; they include people, goods, services, prayers, connections, advocacy, and presence.

The session's first major insight is the Discover Total Resources typology: every community and ministry ecosystem contains four types of resources — People (giving their time and talent), Money (donations, grants, fees), Goods (materials, equipment, space), and Services (professional expertise, technical assistance) — and effective ministry leaders learn to see and access all four rather than defaulting to the grant application as the only legitimate resource-development strategy. Nehemiah is the session's biblical exemplar: before he asked the king for anything, he prayed, he had a clear vision, he had assessed what resources were needed, he understood what motivated the king, and he asked for specific things. Every element of modern resource networking is present in Nehemiah's request.

The session's second major insight is Simon Sinek's Golden Circle: most leaders communicate from the outside in (What → How → Why), but inspiring leaders communicate from the inside out (Why → How → What). The Why is not a description of what the ministry does but of why it exists — the deep conviction that drives it, the difference it exists to make. When participants share their Why compellingly and authentically, they attract partners who share their conviction rather than merely their need.

These concepts are developed through three sequenced activities. First, a Cause Framing worksheet asks participants to draft a two-sentence cause statement and test it against the session's criteria for clarity and resonance — producing a portable statement they can use in real conversations. Second, a network-mapping exercise has participants plot their existing contacts across five stakeholder categories (donors, volunteers, partners, advocates, connectors) and identify at least two gaps, making the abstract idea of a network visible and actionable. Third, a Cold Warm Outreach task asks participants to write a real or simulated introductory message to one identified gap contact, applying the cause-framing language they developed. By the session's end, each participant leaves with a tested cause statement, a personal network map, and one outreach message drafted — artifacts they can carry directly into their ministry contexts.

Session 15

Finding Your People: Resource Networking and the Power of Your Cause

Reframing fundraising as cause-based relationship building — and discovering that the resources your ministry needs already surround you

Marcus had been directing his youth ministry for eleven years when he first heard the phrase "resource development" in a grant-writing workshop. The trainer made it sound simple: write a compelling proposal, submit it to the right foundation, receive the check. Marcus tried it six times over the following three years. Six rejections. His response was not unusual: he quietly concluded that resource development was a specialized skill he simply did not possess, quietly stopped trying, and quietly watched three program positions go unfunded. At the Local Workshop 4 pre-assignment, he read the Biblical Foundation section and paused at Nehemiah 2:1–9. He had read the passage dozens of times. But something new surfaced: Nehemiah had never written a grant proposal. He had prayed, assessed the need, understood his stakeholder, and made a specific, relational ask. He had practiced, in other words, the exact art form that Marcus had been avoiding — not fundraising, but cause-based partnership. That realization did not make the work easy. But it made it possible.

This session dismantles the most paralyzing misconception in ministry resource development — that asking for money defines the work — and replaces it with a robust, research-grounded, biblically-rooted alternative: that resources flow from relationships, relationships flow from trust, and trust flows from a cause so compellingly communicated that it awakens the shared convictions of those who hear it. By the end of this session, you will not just understand that framework. You will have practiced it.

The Misconception That Paralyzes Ministry Leaders

The most common reaction to the phrase "resource development" among DVULI participants is a slight tightening of the shoulders. That physical signal is worth investigating, because it points to a deeply embedded assumption: that resource development means asking people for money they do not want to give, for a cause they do not fully understand, through a relationship that does not yet exist. This assumption is not only psychologically debilitating — it is empirically wrong.

A landmark meta-review of decades of philanthropic research by Bekkers and Wiepking (2011) identified eight mechanisms that consistently drive charitable giving across populations, cultures, and time periods. Two of the strongest and most consistent predictors were values alignment and awareness of need — neither of which requires a formal grant application, a polished PowerPoint, or a development director with a corner office. People give when they discover that your cause expresses something they already believe. Your role is not to convince reluctant strangers to part with money. Your role is to communicate your cause so clearly that the people who already share your convictions can find you.

This reframing has a structural counterpart in the Discover Total Resources guide that anchors the DVULI Local Workshop 4 curriculum. Originally produced by BNY Mellon and distributed to over 500,000 nonprofits, the guide argues that fixating on grants as the primary resource strategy is both strategically narrow and unnecessarily stressful. Resources are not one thing — they are four things. And the four together constitute a picture of community capacity far richer than any grant cycle can capture.

The Total Resources Framework: Four Categories, One Ecosystem

The Total Resources framework identifies four fundamental categories of community resource, each of which is accessible through relationships rather than applications:

  • People — individuals giving their time, talent, expertise, networks, and advocacy. Volunteers, mentors, board members, loaned executives, professional consultants, and intercessors all belong to this category. People are, as the guide puts it, "the source of all resources."
  • Money — donations, grants, membership dues, earned income, and service fees. This is the category that receives disproportionate attention and disproportionate anxiety.
  • Goods — materials, equipment, space, vehicles, and in-kind donations of any physical resource. A congregation that donates its fellowship hall, a local printer who donates paper stock, a contractor who offers surplus lumber — all are providing goods.
  • Services — professional expertise, technical assistance, legal counsel, marketing support, transportation, and any skilled labor provided at reduced or no cost. Services are, according to the guide, "the most underrated of all community resources."

The practical implication of this taxonomy is significant. A ministry leader who is thinking only in terms of monetary donations is scanning roughly 25% of the available resource landscape. The other 75% — the people who will give time, the businesses that will donate goods, the professionals who will offer services — are largely invisible to the leader whose imagination has been colonized by the grant application form.

Asset-Based Community Development (ABCD), the framework developed by Kretzmann and McKnight (1993) that undergirds DVULI's Community Youth Development curriculum, reinforces this point from a different angle. Communities are not catalogues of deficits — they are reservoirs of gifts. Individual talents, local associations, and institutional resources constitute a wealth of community capacity that a needs-based approach systematically fails to see. Mathie and Cunningham (2003) describe ABCD as a fundamentally different epistemology of community: rather than asking "what is broken and who can fix it from outside," asset-based leaders ask "what gifts are here and how do we mobilize them together?" Resource networking, understood through this lens, is not begging — it is asset activation.

Think About It

Before reading on, consider: in the last six months, which of the four resource categories has received the most attention in your ministry's resource development conversations? Which has received the least? What would it mean practically to invest equivalent energy in all four?

Widget 1: Resource Wheel Partnership Mapper

The Resource Wheel is one of the most powerful tools in DVULI's resource networking curriculum. It visualizes the relational structure of your resource network as concentric rings: your innermost circle of known, trusted contributors; the second ring of people and organizations who know your work but don't yet contribute; the third ring of those your inner two rings know; and the outer ring of broader community potential. Use the mapper below to identify and strategize your most valuable second-ring connections.

Resource Wheel Partnership Mapper

Add partner names or organization types to Ring 1 (core contributors) and Ring 2 (those who know your work). Click on the canvas to place them, then generate your outreach strategy.

Nehemiah: The Biblical Exemplar of Resource Networking

The DVULI Local Workshop 4 Biblical Foundation section is built around Nehemiah 2:1–9, and the selection is precise. Nehemiah's encounter with King Artaxerxes is not a prayer meeting — it is a masterclass in cause-based resource networking, and every element of modern partnership development is present in the text.

Consider the sequence. Nehemiah begins in Nehemiah 1 with prayer — not as a preliminary formality, but as an act of intentional dependence that clarifies his vision, grounds his motivation, and shapes his ask. He is not networking in order to build his own platform. He is accessing resources in the service of a calling he has received. This distinction matters because, as Shamir, House, and Arthur (1993) demonstrate in their foundational paper on charismatic leadership, the leaders who inspire genuine, identity-based commitment in others are those who communicate purpose rather than personal ambition. Their research shows that when leaders link organizational mission to followers' core values and moral identity, participation becomes not just something people do but something that expresses who they are. Nehemiah's prayer life ensured his ask would be rooted in that kind of vision.

When the moment arrives with the king, Nehemiah has a clear vision: "the city where my ancestors are buried lies in ruins, and its gates have been destroyed by fire" (Nehemiah 2:3). The specificity is not accidental. He had assessed the situation personally, walking the walls by night in Nehemiah 2:11–15. He knew exactly what was broken. A vague ask — "I need some help with my ministry" — produces a vague response. A precise, assessed ask — "I need letters for the governors, timber from the forest, and safe passage for the journey" — signals seriousness, competence, and trustworthiness.

Nehemiah also demonstrates sophisticated stakeholder understanding. He does not ask the king in a vacuum. He reads the king's emotional state ("the king had never seen him sad before"), he identifies the king's potential motivation ("the king's heart was moved by God"), and he makes the ask at the moment of the king's maximum receptivity. This is not manipulation — it is what the Discover Total Resources guide describes as the fundamental principle of resource development: understanding what is important to potential partners and why they might be appropriate contributors to a particular work. Sargeant and Lee (2004) demonstrated empirically that trust is the single strongest predictor of sustained donor commitment — and trust, in their research, is built through genuine relational knowledge, not transactional solicitation.

"And because the gracious hand of my God was upon me, the king granted my requests." — Nehemiah 2:8 (NIV)

The final element in Nehemiah's model is gratitude and acknowledgment. The text records precisely what was given and by whom. This is not bookkeeping — it is stewardship, and it is the foundation of long-term partnership. Resource relationships that do not cultivate acknowledgment, reporting, and gratitude tend to be one-time transactions rather than sustained partnerships.

The five elements of Nehemiah's model — prayer, clear vision, stakeholder understanding, specific asks, and gratitude — constitute a framework that predates grant writing by 2,500 years, requires no development staff to execute, and is available to every ministry leader in every context, regardless of organizational size or budget.

The Golden Circle: Communicating from the Inside Out

Simon Sinek's 2009 book Start with Why and his widely viewed TED talk, "How Great Leaders Inspire Action," introduce a framework that DVULI's Local Workshop 4 curriculum explicitly incorporates: The Golden Circle. The model is deceptively simple. Most organizations communicate from the outside in: they begin with What they do, explain How they do it, and sometimes — if they get that far — mention Why. Inspiring leaders and organizations invert the sequence. They begin with Why, then describe How, then explain What.

Sinek's formulation is worth quoting directly: "People don't buy what you do; they buy why you do it." The application to ministry resource development is immediate. When a ministry leader walks into a meeting with a potential partner and leads with their program list — "We run a Tuesday tutoring program, a summer camp, and a mentoring initiative for young men ages 12 to 18" — they are leading with What. The potential partner receives a description of activities. When a ministry leader leads instead with their Why — "We believe that every child in this neighborhood deserves at least one adult champion who refuses to give up on them" — they are leading with conviction. The potential partner receives an invitation to share a belief. The difference between those two experiences is the difference between a transaction and a partnership.

The Why, in Sinek's framework, is not a description of what the ministry does — it is a statement of what the ministry believes, what injustice or gap it exists to address, and what transformation it holds possible. It is the answer to the question: "What would be lost if your ministry did not exist?" When that answer is communicated with authenticity and specificity, it performs exactly what Bekkers and Wiepking's (2011) research describes: it allows potential partners who already hold aligned values to recognize themselves in your cause and respond not out of obligation but out of conviction.

The How describes the distinctive approach, values, or methods through which the Why is expressed — the particular ways your ministry embodies its conviction. The What, finally, is the catalog of programs and services: the visible outputs of the Why and How. In the LW4 pre-assignment, participants are guided through the Content and Action Verbs Table and prompted to draft a Why-How-What statement using these three levels. The most common mistake — and the one that the DVULI curriculum explicitly flags — is leading with What. If a partner reads your ministry description and the first three sentences are all about programs and activities, you have communicated the least compelling layer of who you are.

Think About It

Consider the last time you described your ministry to a potential partner or donor. Did you begin with What (your programs), How (your approach), or Why (your conviction)? What would a Why-first version of that description have sounded like? How might the response have differed?

Widget 2: Golden Circle Why-How-What Statement Builder

Use the interactive tool below to build your ministry's Golden Circle statement. Begin at the center — the Why — guided by four sub-questions that help you articulate the deep conviction your ministry exists to express. Then move outward through How and What. The tool will generate a complete statement and flag the most common mistake: leading with What.

Golden Circle Why-How-What Statement Builder

Answer these four sub-questions, then write your Why statement below.

The Neuroscience of Story: Why Narrative Moves People to Action

The instruction to "tell your story" is so common in nonprofit development circles that it has become almost a cliché — and clichés have the unfortunate effect of obscuring the mechanisms they describe. The instruction to tell your story is not a soft suggestion about communication style. It is a recommendation grounded in rigorous neuroscience, and understanding why narrative works the way it does will change how intentionally you craft and deliver your ministry's cause story.

Green and Brock (2000), in their foundational paper introducing the Transportation-Imagery Model, demonstrated experimentally that when audiences become mentally absorbed in a narrative — a state they call transportation — they form stronger beliefs, make fewer counter-arguments against the story's implicit claims, and develop deeper empathic identification with protagonists than audiences who receive the same information in statistical or argumentative form. Transportation is positively correlated with story vividness, character specificity, and emotional engagement. This means that a story about one named young person, in one specific place, at one identifiable moment of crisis — told with sensory detail and emotional honesty — is neurologically more persuasive than the most comprehensive program impact report you could produce.

But persuasion through story is not merely cognitive — it is biological. Barraza and Zak (2009) conducted an experimental neuroscience study in which participants watched a narrative video about a father caring for his terminally ill son. Before and after the viewing, blood draws measured oxytocin levels. Higher narrative-induced oxytocin was a statistically significant predictor of subsequent charitable donation to a cancer charity. The causal chain their research established is worth memorizing: compelling narrative → empathic engagement → oxytocin release → increased generosity. A control group watched a non-narrative video about the same father and son; oxytocin levels did not change, and giving did not increase.

This is the neurochemical mechanism beneath the instruction on page 22 of the LW4 Pre-Assignment, where participants are told that "neurological research has shown that stories motivate voluntary cooperation" and are prompted to identify three events or moments from their ministry that could inspire others to engage. The reason this works — the reason a five-minute cause story told by a youth pastor can unlock partnership that a twenty-page grant proposal cannot — is that the story triggers a biological response in the listener that creates empathic identification and motivational arousal. The listener does not merely understand your cause intellectually. They feel it. And as Bekkers and Wiepking (2011) establish, emotional engagement with a cause is among the strongest predictors of sustained giving and advocacy.

What Makes a Cause Story Effective

Not all stories produce transportation. Green and Brock's research identified several structural features that distinguish transportive narratives from those that fail to engage. The most effective cause stories share five characteristics:

  1. A specific character, time, and place. "A young man named Darnell, on a Tuesday in November, outside a middle school in the third ward" is transportive. "Many young people in our community" is not.
  2. Rising tension. The story must develop a problem that tightens before it resolves. Without tension, transportation does not occur. A story that moves from good to good to good produces no oxytocin.
  3. A moment of crisis or turning point. The climax is the moment of maximum narrative tension — the point at which something irreversible becomes possible. This is where listener hearts engage most deeply.
  4. A resolution that connects to the ministry's Why. The descending action and resolution must make the implicit claim explicit: this transformation happened because of this cause, and this cause needs partners to continue it.
  5. A clear invitation to engage. The best cause stories end not with a conclusion but with a question that opens into participation. "And today, we need three more people who believe what we believe." That sentence transforms a story into an ask.

The LW4 Workbook structures this as the story arc that appears in the "Telling Your Story" section: Introduction, Rising Actions, Climax, Descending Actions, and Resolution. Heather's story from the Kids Kamp case study — the story about her childhood friend Veronica — demonstrates all five characteristics. Note that when she first presented to EBC's board using only program descriptions, the room was polite but unengaged. When she told the story, "five hands went up." The story did what the program presentation could not: it created oxytocin-mediated empathic resonance, it activated values-alignment, and it invited participation rooted in shared conviction rather than institutional need.

"I grew up in a neighborhood just like this one. I met my best friend, Veronica, while playing in the park... I have made the decision to be a champion for those kids, and that is why I do what I do." — Heather Lopez, Kids Kamp case study, DVULI Local Workshop 4 Workbook

There is, however, an important ethical boundary to mark. Simon Sinek is explicit in his writing: "The danger of manipulation is that it works." The neurological power of narrative can be deployed in service of authentic cause communication — helping people who already share your convictions to find you and act on those convictions — or it can be deployed manipulatively, engineering emotional responses to extract commitments that do not reflect genuine alignment. DVULI's framework insists on the former. The cause story is not a fundraising technique for wearing down resistant donors. It is a clarity instrument for finding people who were already looking for you.

Widget 3: Ministry Cause Story Workshop

Building an effective five-minute cause story requires structural scaffolding, not just emotional authenticity. Use the workshop below to draft your story across all five narrative arc elements, test it against four effectiveness criteria, and practice delivering it in exactly five minutes using the built-in timer. Bring your completed story to the Session 15 workshop for peer feedback.

Ministry Cause Story Workshop

Complete all five narrative arc sections, then click "Evaluate My Story" for rubric feedback. Use the 5-minute timer to practice your delivery.

Name a real person (anonymized if needed), a specific time, and a concrete place. Avoid "many young people." Ground the story in a single human moment.

Describe what led to this moment. Build tension. The listener should feel the weight of the circumstances accumulating.

This is the hinge of the story — the moment that could go either way. Lean in. Maximum tension lives here.

What did your ministry do? What changed? Be specific and honest — transformation takes time and rarely follows a straight line.

What does Marcus's story mean? What does it ask of the people listening? End with a clear invitation to engage with your Why.

5:00

From Story to Strategy: Integrating the Three Frameworks

The Total Resources framework, the Golden Circle, and the cause story are not three separate tools. They are three expressions of the same underlying insight: that resources flow from relationships, relationships flow from shared conviction, and shared conviction is activated through authentic, values-centered communication.

Consider how Nehemiah integrates all three. He applies the Total Resources framework by asking for letters (goods: official authorization), timber (goods: materials), and safe passage (services: military escort) — not just for money. He applies the Golden Circle by beginning with Why: "Why should my face not look sad when the city where my ancestors are buried lies in ruins?" That single sentence is a Why statement. It expresses belief, identifies a gap that breaks his heart, names a transformation he believes is possible, and asks implicitly what would be lost if he did not act. And he tells a story: the king is moved because he sees, in Nehemiah's face and in Nehemiah's words, the specific grief of a specific man for a specific city. The story is only a few verses long, but it has a protagonist (Nehemiah), rising tension (the ruined city, the king's question), a climax (the king asks "What do you want?"), and a resolution (the king grants all the requests). Every verse of Nehemiah 2:1–9 is a masterclass in cause story structure.

The integration of these frameworks in your own ministry begins with the same sequence Nehemiah used. Start in prayer — establishing dependence and clarity of vision. Assess the situation personally — walk the walls. Then draft your Golden Circle statement, beginning with Why and moving outward. Then identify the story that makes your Why viscerally real for a listener. Then map your Resource Wheel to identify the partners who might share that Why, and develop a specific, relational approach sequence for each of your most promising second-ring connections.

Sargeant and Lee's (2004) research establishes the endpoint of this process clearly: the goal is not a transaction but a relationship. Donors and partners who feel genuinely known, valued, and connected to a cause demonstrate significantly higher long-term giving, advocacy, and loyalty — outpacing partners acquired through institutional need appeals at every level. The DVULI Resource Wheel is ultimately about building a network of people who feel that your ministry's success is, in some meaningful sense, their own. That outcome is not achievable through a grant application. It is achievable only through the slow, intentional, cause-driven work of relationship building that every session in this curriculum has been preparing you to do.

Think About It

Looking at the five elements of Nehemiah's model — prayer, assessed vision, stakeholder understanding, specific ask, gratitude and stewardship — which element is currently strongest in your resource networking practice? Which is most underdeveloped? What would it look like to invest focused attention in your weakest element over the next thirty days?

Practical Checklist: Preparing for Your Resource Networking Conversations

The following checklist synthesizes the key preparatory steps from the Total Resources framework, the Golden Circle, and the cause story workshop into a sequence you can apply before any partnership conversation. Whether you are meeting with a potential major donor, a church pastor, a local business owner, or a foundation program officer, this sequence applies.

  • Clarify your Why. Can you state, in two sentences, the conviction that drives your ministry — without mentioning a single program? If not, return to your Golden Circle statement builder.
  • Identify the resource category. Before the meeting, be specific: are you seeking people (volunteers, board members, loaned expertise), money (donation, grant, sponsorship), goods (equipment, space, materials), or services (professional assistance, technical support)? The specificity of your ask signals preparation and respect.
  • Research your potential partner's values and interests. What does this person or organization care about? Where does their Why overlap with yours? Bekkers and Wiepking's (2011) research confirms that values alignment is the most consistent predictor of giving. Find the overlap.
  • Prepare your cause story. Have one specific, vivid, five-minute story ready that communicates your Why through a human experience. Practice it until it feels natural, not rehearsed.
  • Identify your warm introduction path. Review your Resource Wheel: is there someone in your inner or second ring who knows this potential partner and could make a personal introduction? A warm introduction multiplies the probability of a positive response far more than a cold outreach.
  • Make a specific ask. Know in advance exactly what you are asking for: not "support" but "two hours per week of volunteer mentoring for the next six months." Specificity honors the other person's time and increases the likelihood of a clear, usable response.
  • Plan your follow-up. Know before you leave the meeting how and when you will follow up. Send a handwritten or personal note of gratitude within 24 hours of every conversation, regardless of outcome.

Facilitator Guide: Session 15 Workshop Delivery Notes

Session Objectives

By the end of Session 15, participants will be able to: (1) distinguish between the four resource categories in the Total Resources framework and apply that taxonomy to their own ministry context; (2) articulate their ministry's Why using the Golden Circle framework; (3) deliver a five-minute cause story that meets all four effectiveness criteria; and (4) identify and develop a specific outreach strategy for their three most promising second-ring Resource Wheel connections.

Pre-Workshop Preparation

  • Participants should have completed the LW4 Pre-Assignment in full, including the Golden Circle Why-How-What statement, the Resource Wheel exercise, and the cause story draft (three story events/moments from the Pre-Assignment page 22).
  • Ask participants to bring a printed copy of their Why-How-What statement and their story draft to the session.
  • Prepare three to four pairs for the story feedback exercise (Personal Story Feedback Form, LW4 Workbook page 32).
  • Have the LW4 Workbook Resource Wheel (page 24), Resource Wall (pages 26–27), and Prospective Partner Profile Form (page 28) accessible for reference during breakout activities.

Suggested Sequence and Timing

  • Opening (15 min): Debrief the hook scenario and surface participants' prior assumptions about resource development. Ask: "What word comes to mind when you hear 'fundraising'?" Use responses to frame the session's reframing work.
  • Total Resources Framework (25 min): Group activity using LW4 Workbook "Resources that Make a Difference" (page 21). Each group takes one resource category and generates three concrete examples from their ministry context. Full-room share-out.
  • Golden Circle Review and Partner Feedback (30 min): Pairs share their Why-How-What statements (10 min per pair). Feedback focuses on: Does the statement begin with Why? Is the Why conviction-based or program-based? Is the How distinctive? Partners record feedback in the Your Statement Table (LW4 Pre-Assignment page 20).
  • Cause Story Practice (45 min): Each participant delivers their cause story to a partner using the Personal Story Feedback Form (LW4 Workbook page 32) as the rubric. Facilitator models the story arc with Heather Lopez's story from the Kids Kamp case study before individual practice begins.
  • Resource Wheel and Outreach Strategy (30 min): Small groups of three to four map their Resource Wheels and identify their three most promising second-ring connections. Groups use the Prospective Partner Profile Form (LW4 Workbook page 28) to develop an approach sequence for each.
  • Debrief and Close (15 min): Full-room debrief of the day's learning. Ask: "What one thing shifted in how you think about resource development?" Connect back to Nehemiah 2:1–9 and the DVULI Breakthrough Plan's Relationships category (Breakthrough Handbook, Step 4).

Manager Debrief Tips

  • Watch for participants who conflate Why statements with mission statements. Mission statements describe what an organization does; Why statements express the conviction that drives it. Redirect as needed.
  • In story feedback pairs, watch for the tendency to give purely positive feedback. Encourage use of the "I think your story would be stronger if..." framing from the LW5 Pre-Assignment guidance on constructive feedback.
  • Some participants will initially resist the cause story exercise as "manipulative." Use Sinek's ethical boundary — "We use stories to connect with individuals that God has already given a passion or heart for our Why" (LW4 Pre-Assignment, page 22) — to address this concern directly.
  • The Resource Wheel activity is most productive when participants are specific: not "local churches" but "Pastor Williams at Ebenezer Baptist" — a person with a name, a known set of values, and a relationship history.

Key Takeaways

  • Resource development is not fundraising — it is cause-based relationship building in which the Why is communicated so clearly that partners who already share your conviction can recognize themselves in your work and respond.
  • The Total Resources framework identifies four resource categories — People, Money, Goods, and Services — and effective ministry leaders scan all four rather than defaulting to the grant application as the only legitimate resource development strategy.
  • Nehemiah 2:1–9 models a five-element resource networking sequence — prayer, assessed vision, stakeholder understanding, specific ask, and gratitude — that anticipates every principle of modern cause-based partnership development.
  • Simon Sinek's Golden Circle demonstrates that inspiring leaders communicate from the inside out (Why → How → What), attracting partners based on shared conviction rather than institutional need; the most common mistake is leading with What.
  • Neurological research (Green & Brock, 2000; Barraza & Zak, 2009) confirms that narrative creates empathic engagement through the oxytocin pathway, producing measurable increases in voluntary giving and participation that statistical or programmatic appeals cannot replicate.
  • Sargeant and Lee's (2004) research establishes trust as the single strongest predictor of sustained donor commitment, which means that long-term resource partnerships are built through genuine relational knowledge and shared conviction — not transactional solicitation.
  • The five-minute cause story — built on a specific character, rising tension, a climactic turning point, ministry involvement, and a clear invitation to engage — is the primary vehicle through which the Golden Circle's Why is communicated in a way that moves people to action.

Looking Ahead

Session 16 moves from resource networking into the final integrative work of the DVULI journey: accelerating change through collaborative leadership, Breakthrough Plan presentation, and a transition into the alumni community. You will bring your completed Why-How-What statement and your cause story — refined from the feedback you receive in this session — into the Breakthrough presentation, where they will form the foundation of your Ministry Focus Statement's communication strategy. Begin now by reviewing your Ministry Learning Journal and asking: in the resource networking area, what one capacity development step, taken in the next thirty days, would most accelerate your path to Breakthrough?
